element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Foundation for all subsequent life—must maintain balance within ranges for multicellular life
Critical parameter affecting biome viability; extremes cause ocean boil-off or ice age scenarios
Spatial distribution affects species isolation and diversification

element_introduction_order.first_appears: Initial life placement, treated specially in system
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Foundation tier; enable evolution pathways to multicellular organisms

element_introduction_order.element: Multicellular animal taxa (radiates, molluscs, fishes, birds, cetaceans)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Require balanced environmental parameters; subject to mass extinction events
Foundation for food chains; daisies in Daisyworld adjust color relative to temperature
Final challenge: evolve sapient species (e.g., sapient radiates) to reach advanced civilization
Highest technology tier; unlocks machine life as easter egg upon nuclear destruction
Ultimate goal in Random Planet mode; represents successful planetary stewardship

element_introduction_order.first_appears: Available as interventionist tools throughout gameplay
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Player tools for planet engineering; cost energy units based on planetary development
Increases lifeform intelligence through external influence; reference to 2001: A Space Odyssey

element_introduction_order.element: Natural disasters (hurricanes, wildfires, volcanic eruptions, earthquakes, tsunamis)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Environmental hazards with cascading effects; increase atmospheric dust, trigger tsunamis, etc.

element_introduction_order.element: Civilization-dependent disasters (disease, pollution, nuclear war, nuclear winter)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Population-dependent threats; nuclear fuel shortage can trigger catastrophic war

element_introduction_order.element: Global warming and sea level rise
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Melting ice caps; extreme heat can evaporate oceans entirely

element_introduction_order.first_appears: Can occur unpredictably; often followed by diversification
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Resets evolutionary branches; enables experimentation with new species and ecosystems

element_introduction_order.first_appears: Available if planet overheats and loses all water
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Can restore oceans; helps recover from extreme planetary conditions

element_introduction_order.first_appears: Hidden; appears if highest-tech city (Nanotech Age) destroyed by nuclear explosion
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Easter egg reward for triggering specific destructive event chain

typical_relief_triggers: Successful species adaptation to environmental conditions
Mass extinction followed by new diversification opportunity
Restoring oceans via ice comet intervention
typical_tension_triggers: Unpredictable species thriving or dying for unclear reasons
Global warming causing ice cap melt and sea level rise
Nuclear war threat from fuel shortage or rising militarization
Mass extinction events resetting evolutionary progress
Planetary conditions falling outside viable ranges for multicellular life
Energy budget constraints limiting terraforming interventions
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Open-ended, asynchronous pacing driven by player experimentation and planetary simulation. Crises emerge unpredictably; players can pause and adjust parameters at will. No time pressure in Random Planet mode; Scenario mode introduces specific goal-driven objectives with implicit urgency.

spatial_grammar.secret_convention: Machine life easter egg hidden behind high-tech civilization destruction cascade

difficulty_curve.early_game: Low friction: player establishes baseline planetary parameters (atmosphere, temperature, continents) and introduces simple life forms. Feedback is immediate and visual.
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Moderate complexity: multicellular life evolution introduces unpredictability ('species may thrive or die for no apparent reason'). Environmental parameters must be continuously adjusted. Mass extinction events force adaptive strategy.
difficulty_curve.late_game: High challenge: sentient life evolution and civilization advancement introduce interdependent systems (technology, pollution, nuclear weapons). Final goal of achieving planetary exodus requires balancing all subsystems successfully. Scenarios like 'Earth 2XXX' add adversarial elements (self-replicating robots, nuclear warfare).
notable_spikes: Transition from single-celled to multicellular life (requires precise environmental balance)
Emergence of sapient species and civilization (introduces technology and disaster chains)
Late-game scenario: 'Earth 2XXX' (rescue civilization from robots and nuclear annihilation; option to trigger great flood)

notable_level_patterns.description: Eight structured scenarios with specific goals: Aquarium and Cambrian Earth (Earth evolution stages), 1990 Earth (contemporary management), Mars/Venus/Ice Planet/Dune (terraforming challenges), Earth 2XXX (crisis management with options). Progression from familiar Earth to alien planets to future dystopia.

Daisyworld biome mode isolates single ecosystem and applies James Lovelock's Daisyworld model where daisies self-regulate planet temperature by adjusting color. Teaches systems-level planetary self-regulation.

Open-ended mode without mandatory goals. Player has unlimited or budget-based energy to terraform and evolve life. Challenge is player-defined: successfully evolve sentient civilization and achieve planetary exodus before 10-billion-year heat death of sun.

Late-game scenarios emphasize interdependent failure chains: nuclear fuel shortage → nuclear war → nuclear winter; global warming → ocean boil-off → loss of all water; high-tech city destruction → machine life emergence (easter egg). Single decision can trigger cascade of consequences.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Earth 2XXX (robots, nuclear war, optional great flood trigger)
